title: Comedy
subtitle: Cerebral, dry, dark, formally inventive comedy. Intelligence over volume.

sections.name: I. High Priority — Coen Brothers
sections.desc: You said yes to Big Lebowski / Burn After Reading / Hail Caesar territory. Here's the comedic Coens canon, prioritized.

items.title: The Big Lebowski
items.whyPriority: The Coens' most beloved. Bowling, White Russians, Sam Elliott narrating, Bridges as the Dude. Stoner Chandler. Watch if you haven't, rewatch if you have.
items.pitch: The Dude wants his rug back. Then a kidnapping. Then a severed toe. Then a marmot.

critics.quote: Critically mixed in 1998; now considered one of the great American comedies.

items.title: Burn After Reading
items.whyPriority: Coens' nihilist espionage farce. Pitt, Clooney, McDormand, Malkovich. The CIA mostly confused. The ending is the joke.
items.pitch: Two gym employees find a CD with what appears to be CIA secrets. They try to extort the owner. Things escalate to no purpose.

critics.quote: Polarizing initially; widely beloved now. Brad Pitt's strongest comedic performance.

items.title: Hail, Caesar!
items.whyPriority: Coens' love letter to 1950s Hollywood. Brolin as a fixer at a major studio. Tatum tap dancing. Ehrenreich's 'Would that it were so simple' scene is classic.
items.pitch: A studio fixer in 1951 Hollywood deals with kidnappings, pregnancies, communism, and a mediocre Western star.

critics.quote: Critically warm. 'Would that it were so simple' became a cult phrase.

items.title: Fargo
items.whyPriority: Coens at their tonal peak — comedy and brutality intertwined. McDormand as Marge Gunderson. Won Best Original Screenplay and Best Actress.
items.pitch: A car salesman hires two men to kidnap his wife so he can collect ransom from her father. A pregnant police chief gets involved.

critics.quote: Won 2 Oscars. Seven nominations including Best Picture and Best Director.

items.title: O Brother, Where Art Thou?
items.pitch: Loose Odyssey adaptation set in Depression-era Mississippi. Clooney leading three escaped convicts. The soundtrack outsold the film and won Album of the Year.

critics.quote: Two Oscar nominations including Best Adapted Screenplay.

items.title: Raising Arizona
items.pitch: Early Coens. Cage and Holly Hunter as a couple who can't have children, so they steal one. Manic, slapstick, original.

critics.quote: Initially divisive; aged into respect. Considered the launching pad for Cage's idiosyncratic period.

sections.name: II. High Priority — Wes Anderson (the deeper cuts)
sections.desc: You've seen 'a lot' — listed for completeness with priority on the ones often overlooked.

items.title: The Grand Budapest Hotel
items.pitch: Anderson's most accessible. Ralph Fiennes as the concierge. Multiple frame ratios, multiple time periods. Won 4 Oscars.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars. Tied with Birdman for most wins that year.

items.title: The French Dispatch
items.whyPriority: Anderson's most divisive recent work. Anthology of New Yorker-style stories. Dense, demanding, rewards attention.
items.pitch: Anthology of stories from a fictional American magazine in a fictional French city. Anderson at his most magazine-like.

critics.quote: Polarizing. Some critics called it overstuffed; others called it a masterpiece.

items.title: Rushmore
items.pitch: Anderson's second. Bill Murray's career resurrection. Schwartzman's debut.

critics.quote: Generally considered Anderson's first masterpiece. Murray won several critics' awards.

sections.name: III. High Priority — Satire
sections.desc: Network, Wag the Dog territory. Where comedy meets political weight.

items.title: Network
items.whyPriority: Paddy Chayefsky's screenplay. Predicted reality TV, infotainment, and contemporary cable news with horrifying accuracy. 'I'm mad as hell.' Won 4 Oscars.
items.pitch: A network newscaster's on-air breakdown becomes a ratings sensation. The network exploits him. Faye Dunaway is terrifying.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars including Best Original Screenplay (Chayefsky), Best Actor (Finch), Best Actress (Dunaway), Best Supporting Actress (Straight).

items.title: Dr. Strangelove
items.whyPriority: You've seen all of Kubrick. Pre-marked Watched in case you have, but listed because it's the satirical foundation everything else owes a debt to.
items.pitch: Cold War nuclear war as black comedy. Sellers in three roles.

critics.quote: Routinely listed among the greatest comedies and greatest American films ever made.

items.title: Wag the Dog
items.whyPriority: Hoffman and De Niro as a Hollywood producer and political fixer fabricating a fake war to distract from a presidential scandal. Released weeks before Lewinsky broke. Eerie.
items.pitch: A spin doctor and a Hollywood producer manufacture a war to distract from a presidential sex scandal. Released right before Lewinsky.

critics.quote: Two Oscar nominations. Mamet co-wrote the screenplay.

items.title: Don't Look Up
items.pitch: Two scientists discover an extinction-level comet. Nobody listens. McKay's despair-comedy about climate change, dressed as something else.

critics.quote: Critically polarizing. Audiences split sharply. Whether it lands depends on your tolerance for the technique.

items.title: Idiocracy
items.whyPriority: Mike Judge's prophetic dystopian satire. Fox barely released it. Has aged from absurd to documentary.
items.pitch: Average soldier wakes up 500 years in the future and finds himself the smartest person alive.

critics.quote: Originally dumped to a few cities; cult resurrection through DVD. Now widely cited as eerily prescient.

items.title: Election
items.pitch: High school student council election as Clinton-era political allegory. Witherspoon's career-defining role. Broderick at his most pathetic.

critics.quote: Two Oscar nominations. One of Witherspoon's defining performances.

sections.name: IV. British Dry
sections.desc: Ealing through In the Loop. Wit instead of slapstick.

items.title: In the Loop
items.whyPriority: Iannucci's spinoff from The Thick of It. Bureaucratic farce around the Iraq War. Peter Capaldi's profanity is poetry.
items.pitch: British and American bureaucrats stumble toward war while saying things they shouldn't and ducking each other.

critics.quote: Oscar nomination for Best Adapted Screenplay. Cult devotion among political-comedy fans.

items.title: Death at a Funeral (2007)
items.whyPriority: British original (not the 2010 American remake). Family secrets unravel during a father's funeral. Genuinely funny in classic British escalation pattern.
items.pitch: An Englishman's funeral is interrupted by hallucinogens, a dwarf, and a dead body that won't stay still.

critics.quote: British original strongly preferred over the American remake by most critics.

items.title: Kind Hearts and Coronets
items.whyPriority: Ealing classic. Alec Guinness plays eight roles. Distant heir methodically murders his way to a dukedom. The blueprint of British dry.
items.pitch: A penniless heir murders his way through eight relatives to inherit a title.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the greatest British comedies. AFI/BFI canon.

items.title: Withnail and I
items.whyPriority: Cult British classic. Two failed actors take a holiday in the Lake District. Richard E. Grant's debut. Quotable to obsessive degrees.
items.pitch: 1969 London. Two unemployed actors flee to a country cottage owned by an eccentric uncle. Things go wrong.

critics.quote: Cult devotion. Routinely listed in British greatest-of polls. The script was Robinson's reflection on his own 1960s.

items.title: The Death of Stalin
items.whyPriority: Iannucci does Soviet succession. Pitch-black political farce. Buscemi as Khrushchev. Banned in Russia.
items.pitch: 1953. Stalin dies. The men around him scramble to seize power while pretending they aren't scrambling.

critics.quote: BAFTA Best Actor (Comedy) for Simon Russell Beale. Critically acclaimed.

items.title: This Is Spinal Tap
items.whyPriority: The mockumentary template. 'These go to eleven.' Christopher Guest, Michael McKean, Harry Shearer. Aged into universal cultural touchstone.
items.pitch: Documentary crew follows a fading British metal band on a disastrous American tour.

critics.quote: Cultural earthquake. Influence on comedy filmmaking is still ongoing.

items.title: What We Do in the Shadows (2014)
items.whyPriority: Three vampires share a flat in Wellington. Pre-FX series. Combines your vampire interest with mockumentary form. Specific and joyful.
items.pitch: Documentary about flatmate vampires. The TV series followed.

critics.quote: Built sustained cult following; spawned the FX series. Considered one of the strongest 2010s comedies.

items.title: Best in Show
items.whyPriority: Guest's mockumentary about a dog show. Largely improvised. Catherine O'Hara, Eugene Levy, Fred Willard. Quotable to the same obsessive degree as Spinal Tap.
items.pitch: Five families compete in a national dog show. The dogs are normal.

critics.quote: Most beloved of the Guest mockumentaries by most counts.

items.title: Waiting for Guffman
items.pitch: Small Missouri town puts on a community theater production celebrating its sesquicentennial. Guest, O'Hara, Levy, Willard.

critics.quote: Cult classic. Established Guest's career as a mockumentary filmmaker.
